Load when the task is analytic (compute, measure, process data) and the skill covers the API/library you'll use; for descriptive tasks (design, explain, survey, plan), you can often answer directly without loading.

Keyword pre-scan — top lexical matches from a much larger catalog (word-overlap only, synonym-blind). For a task request, one on-point hit here usually suffices. For "what can you do in X" questions it doesn't — fan `search_skills` across the field's own terms first, then answer from the union. Only name skills actually retrieved this conversation; don't fill gaps from training.
- chime-scint-artifact-guards — Run and visualize the CHIME scintillation artifact-control guards in the Faber2026 Lorentzian driver. Use when fitting CHIME-band FRB scintillation, checking whether a CHIME decorrelation scale is real or an upchannelization artifact, applying the harmonic (coarse-channel comb) mask, running the off-pulse ACF null and low-lag excision stability checks, or auditing the fail-closed provenance gate across CHIME configs. Produces the three-panel guard-diagnostics figure and the provenance-gate matrix.
- product-self-knowledge — Stop and consult this skill whenever your response would include specific facts about Anthropic's products. Covers: Claude Code (how to install, Node.js requirements, platform/OS support, MCP server integration, configuration), Claude API (function calling/tool use, batch processing, SDK usage, rate limits, pricing, models, streaming), and Claude.ai (Pro vs Team vs Enterprise plans, feature limits). Trigger this even for coding tasks that use the Anthropic SDK, content creation mentioning Claude capabilities or pricing, or LLM provider comparisons. Any time you would otherwise rely on memory for Anthropic product details, verify here instead — your training data may be outdated or wrong.
- hardening-research-code — Use when research or scientific code must be trustworthy — verifiably correct, regression-safe, and numerically stable. Triggers: harden this code, is this numerically correct, add regression tests vs known results, make the research code robust.
- cli-vs-mcp-routing — Decide whether to perform an operation via an MCP tool call or a shell/CLI command. Use this whenever both surfaces could plausibly accomplish a task — querying a service that has both an MCP server and a CLI (GitHub, Sanity, Cloudflare, gh/git, package managers, cloud SDKs), running mutations against external state, or composing multi-step workflows that touch both local files and remote services. Trigger even when the user doesn't name the dilemma: if you're about to reach for an MCP tool and a CLI would also work (or vice versa), consult this first. Especially load-bearing for autonomous/unattended runs where a wrong mutation compounds.
- astropy-fundamentals — Work with astronomical data using AstroPy for FITS file I/O, coordinate transformations, physical units, precise time handling, catalog cross-matching, photutils photometry, and specutils spectroscopy.
- ensuring-reproducibility — Use when a result, experiment, or analysis must be reproducible by others or by a future session. Triggers: make this reproducible, capture provenance, pin the environment for this result, why can't I reproduce X.
- domain-modeling — Build and sharpen a project's domain model. Use when the user wants to pin down domain terminology or a ubiquitous language, record an architectural decision, or when another skill needs to maintain the domain model.

project:proj_55f9c893cfe1
  - [47 minutes ago] [observed] oran is the one burst with high on-pulse bright-channel power share (~21%), but it is NOT RFI: of 1448 bright on-pulse channels only 242 (3% of ACF power) are persistent (also bright off-pulse); the other 1206 (18.6%) are bright ONLY during the 74 ms burst = intrinsic burst spectral structure. Masking them would destroy real signal. oran also required the time-median flat-field fallback (off-pulse window <50 bins) since it is the widest burst.  [mem_d88ce6e5376d]
  - [17 minutes ago] [observed] DM diagnostic figure generator lives in the pipeline submodule at dispersion/dm_campaign/render_dm_zoom_comparison.py; it reads the campaign fits.json and dedisperses each burst's raw Stokes-I waterfall for display. Two modes: --mode adopted (each band at its own DM, 2 cols) and --mode discriminate (each band at both candidate DMs, 4 cols). Run from pipeline root with PYTHONPATH=. (dispersion is a namespace pkg with no __init__) using /Users/jakobfaber/.conda/envs/py312/bin/python -m dispersion.dm_campaign.render_dm_zoom_comparison. It vendors crop_on_pulse/normalise_channels verbatim from dm_joint_phase (which is NOT in the current pin) and imports shift_waterfall_residual_dm, _freq_grid_mhz, _orient_waterfall_to_ascending_frequency, CHIME_DT_S=2.56e-6, DSA_DT_S=3.2768e-5 from dispersion.dm_power_analysis.  [mem_59e5b6afb9e3]
  - [6 days ago] [observed] When replaying a burst-fit promotion patch across pipeline lineages, CAMPAIGN_REPORT.md predictably conflicts in exactly two regions (the suffix-map table and the two-screen table) because johndoeII rows differ between lineages. Resolve zach-only: keep the target pin's johndoeII placement/values, apply only the promoted burst's changes.  [mem_8e6c7b161375]
  - [26 minutes ago] [observed] For wide CHIME bursts whose chosen off-pulse window is <50 bins (mahi, oran, johndoeII, isha), the time-median flat-field must exclude the on-pulse columns when estimating per-channel gain — otherwise a wide burst (oran, 74 ms, fills ~half the record) biases the median and leaves a residual coarse-channel comb in the top subband ACF.  [mem_2dbc291c80d7]
  - [1 hour ago] [observed] Per-band DM precision quantified from analysis/dm-joint-phase-v2/results/fits.json: CHIME phase-coherence peaks are narrower than DSA in all 12 sightlines, with the per-band uncertainty ratio sigma_DSA/sigma_CHIME running 1.4x (freya, phineas) to 17x (oran), median 7.4x. The two sightlines with a real between-band DM offset exceeding quoted band errors are isha (dDM=-0.117) and phineas (+0.057 pc/cm3).  [mem_a3e823500405]
  - [1 hour ago] [observed] fits.json (in analysis/dm-joint-phase-v2/results/) is a list of 12 per-burst dicts, each with keys burst, chime, dsa, joint. Per-band dicts carry dm, sigma, sigma_components (jackknife/resolution/cutoff/floor, floor=0.005), and selected_curve (dm_grid, score = the raw phase-coherence curve). joint carries chime_minus_dsa, between_band_sigma, difference_sigma, tension_sigma, joint_q. This is the source for any DM diagnostic figure.  [mem_eb88876d0367]
  (showing 6 of 113 on record)

## user
We need to make sure our ToA analysis rests on a robust definition of the ToA. I think we're using the peak value time bin in the on-pulse region of the timeseries right now. I think a better ToA definition would be the ToA of the burst *model* that we fit in the joint modeling (the 2D time-freq fits).

> **tool_result**: {"stdout":"=== model __call__ (713-800) ===\n    def __call__(\n        self, p: FRBParams, model_key: str = \"M3\", freq_subset: NDArray[np.bool_] | None = None\n    ) -> NDArray[np.floating]:\n        \"\"\"Return model dynamic spectrum for parameters *p*.\n\n        Args:\n            p: Parameters\n            model_key: Model variant\n            freq_subset: Optional boolean mask of frequencies to evaluate.\n                         If None, evaluates all frequencies.\n        \"\"\"\n        if freq_subset is None:\n            freq = self.freq\n        else:\n            freq = self.freq[freq_subset]\n\n        n_freq = freq.size\n        n_time = self.time.size\n\n        ref_freq = np.median(self.freq)  # Keep global ref_freq for consistency\n        amp = p.c0 * (freq / ref_freq) ** p.gamma\n\n        # Dispersion delay\n        dd_full = self._dispersion_delay(p.delta_dm)\n        if freq_subset is None:\n            mu = p.t0 + dd_full[:, None]\n        else:\n            mu = p.t0 + dd_full[freq_subset, None]\n\n        # Intra-channel smearing scales with the TRUE DM offset from the de-smear\n        # baseline: dm_init (the DM the data was dedispersed at) plus the fitted\n        # residual delta_dm. With dm_init=0 in the configs, omitting delta_dm here\n        # left a fitted residual DM (e.g. CHIME delta_dm~4.7) with zero modelled\n        # smearing, so its real smearing leaked into zeta/tau and biased alpha\n        # (Codex review, 2026-06). hypot() squares sig_dm, so a negative residual\n        # gives the same |ΔDM| smearing magnitude.\n        dm_smear = self.dm_init + p.delta_dm\n        if model_key in {\"M1\", \"M3\"}:\n            sig_full = self._smearing_sigma(dm_smear, p.zeta)\n        else:\n            sig_full = self._smearing_sigma(dm_smear, 0.0)\n\n        if freq_subset is None:\n            sig = sig_full[:, None]\n        else:\n            sig = sig_full[freq_subset, None]\n\n        # Guard against non-physical width\n

## assistant
I've traced the full ToA path. Here's my assessment — you're right that this needs a firmer footing, and the reasons are stronger than just "more precise."

## What the ToA is right now

The current ToA is a **single peak sample** of the frequency-collapsed, dedispersed intensity profile. In `crossmatching/chime_singlebeam.py`, `_collapse_and_peak()` noise-normalizes each channel, sums over frequency, Savitzky-Golay smooths (window 9, order 3), and takes `np.nanargmax` → `peak_index`. That index becomes the native ToA in `toa_400_from_peak()`, then `compute_toa()` (in `toa_crossmatch.py`) refers it to 400 MHz via the cold-plasma shift ($K_{\rm DM}=4148.808$). Same convention on both bands; the CHIME−DSA offset is the difference of the two 400-MHz-referred peak times.

Two properties of this definition are problematic, and they're separate issues:

**1. The peak of a scattered profile is not the arrival time — and the bias is band-dependent.** The scattered profile is (intrinsic Gaussian) ⊗ (pulse-broadening function with timescale $\tau$). The convolution's peak is pulled *later* than the true burst time by an amount that grows with $\tau$. Since $\tau \propto \nu^{-\alpha}$ (α≈4), CHIME at 0.6 GHz is broadened ~25–40× more than DSA at 1.4 GHz. So the CHIME peak is displaced from the true arrival by far more than the DSA peak, and **that differential displacement lands directly in your measured CHIME−DSA offset as a scattering systematic.** Referring both to 400 MHz corrects the *dispersive* delay but does nothing about the *scattering* delay — that's a different physical effect the current pipeline never removes.

**2. The error budget contains no localization term.** `error_chime_ms`/`error_dsa_ms` come only from `calculate_dm_timing_error()` — purely the DM-uncertainty referral term. There is no contribution from how well the peak itself is localized. For chromatica the quoted `error_chime_ms` is 5 µs (DM-only); the actual peak-pick uncertainty on a smoothed, noisy, scattered profile is not represented at all.

## Why the model t0 is the right fix

The joint 2D fits already produce exactly what you want. In `burstfit.py` the model is `mu = t0 + dispersion_delay(ν)`, and that Gaussian is convolved with the scattering PBF. So **`t0` is the intrinsic (pre-scattering) burst centroid** — the arrival time with the scattering tail deconvolved out, not the scattered peak. The joint fit JSONs (`analysis/scattering-refit-2026-06/joint_json/*_joint_fit.json`) already store it per band with full posteriors:

- `percentiles/t0_C` and `percentiles/t0_D` (median + asymmetric bounds)
- For chromatica: `t0_C = 28.4630` ms (−0.0044/+0.0048), `t0_D = 28.5366` ms (−0.0072/+0.0073)

So switching to the model ToA gives you (a) a scatter-corrected arrival time, removing the band-dependent systematic in issue #1, and (b) a genuine posterior width for the localization error, fixing issue #2. Both bands are fit under a shared $\tau$/$\alpha$ but independent per-band $t_0$, which is exactly the structure you want for a differential offset.

## The one real subtlety before we wire it in

The fitted `t0` is in **milliseconds on each band's cropped/centred/downsampled time axis** (`io.py`: `_centre_burst` rolls the profile peak to mid-record, `_crop_on_pulse` trims, then `time = arange(n_t)*dt_ms`). It is *not* an absolute UTC time. The peak-bin pipeline sidesteps this because it tracks the absolute sample index straight from the singlebeam `time0`. To make the model ToA a drop-in replacement we have to **propagate the absolute-time anchor through the crop/centre/downsample chain** so `t0` (axis-ms) maps back to native sample → `time0` → `compute_toa`. That's the actual implementation work; the physics and the posteriors are already done.

There's also a definitional choice hiding in "the model ToA," and it's worth pinning explicitly:

- **intrinsic centroid `t0`** (pre-scattering) — physically cleanest; this is what removes the scattering systematic
- **model profile peak** (argmax of the *scattered* model) — matches the current convention's intent but keeps the scatter bias, just measured noiselessly
- **model first moment** (centroid of the scattered profile) — yet another value

I'd recommend the intrinsic `t0`, because the whole point is to get an arrival time that isn't contaminated by band-dependent scattering. But if any of these offsets need to stay consistent with externally published CHIME/FRB TOAs (which use their own peak/S-G convention), that consistency argument might push toward the scattered-peak definition instead.

Before I build anything, two questions:
> **tool_use** `ask_user`
> ```json
> {"human_description": "Asking which model-time definition to adopt", "question": "Which point on the fitted model should define the ToA?", "header": "ToA definition", "options": [{"label": "Intrinsic centroid t0 (Recommended)", "description": "The pre-scattering Gaussian centroid, per band, directly from the joint fit posteriors.", "pros": "Removes the band-dependent scattering systematic; physically the true arrival time; posterior width gives a real localization error.", "cons": "Differs from the current peak convention by a known scatter offset, so absolute values shift; may not match externally-published CHIME peak TOAs."}, {"label": "Scattered model profile peak", "description": "argmax of the full scattered model profile (intrinsic Gaussian convolved with the PBF).", "pros": "Matches the intent of the current peak-bin convention but measured noise-free from the model; stays comparable to externally-published peak TOAs.", "cons": "Retains the band-dependent scattering delay in the CHIME-DSA offset \u2014 does not fix issue #1."}, {"label": "Report both t0 and model peak", "description": "Adopt intrinsic t0 as primary, tabulate the scattered-peak ToA alongside as a cross-check column.", "pros": "Transparent about the scatter offset; lets reviewers see the systematic explicitly.", "cons": "More columns/plumbing; need to be clear which one drives the offset analysis."}]}
> ```

> **tool_result**: {"stdout":"=== batch_codetection_data.py (how cubes + anchors are built) head ===\n#!/usr/bin/env python\n\"\"\"Render connected CHIME+gap+DSA observation figures for all co-detections.\n\nUses raw .npy cubes (not joint-fit models). TOA alignment from\ncrossmatching/toa_crossmatch_results.json.\n\n  FLITS_DATA=~/Data/Faber2026/dsa110 python batch_codetection_data.py [out-dir]\n\nDefault out-dir: ../figures/prototypes/codetection (Faber2026, gitignored).\n\"\"\"\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport sys\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport matplotlib\n\nmatplotlib.use(\"Agg\")\nimport matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n\nREPO = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]\nMS_ROOT = REPO.parent\nsys.path.insert(0, str(REPO))\nsys.path.insert(0, str(REPO / \"scattering\"))\n\nfrom flits.batch.codetection_data import BURST_ORDER, load_codetection_bands  # noqa: E402\nfrom flits.batch.codetection_plots import plot_codetection_observations  # noqa: E402\n\n\ndef _save(fig, out_dir: Path, stem: str) -> None:\n    out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    for ext, kw in (\n        (\"png\", dict(dpi=200)),\n        (\"pdf\", dict(bbox_inches=\"tight\")),\n        (\"svg\", dict(bbox_inches=\"tight\")),\n    ):\n        fp = out_dir / f\"{stem}.{ext}\"\n        fig.savefig(fp, **kw)\n        print(f\"wrote {fp}\")\n\n\ndef main() -> None:\n    out_dir = Path(sys.argv[1]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else MS_ROOT / \"figures/prototypes/codetection\"\n    ok, fail = [], []\n    for burst in BURST_ORDER:\n        try:\n            bands = load_codetection_bands(burst)\n            fig = plot_codetection_observations(\n                bands,\n                per_band_scale=True,\n                gap_label=False,\n                figsize=(4.8, 4.9),\n            )\n            _save(fig, out_dir, f\"{burst}_codetection\")\n            plt.close(fig)\n            ok.append(burst)\n        except Exception as exc:\n            print(f\"FAIL {burst}: {exc}\", file=sys.stderr)\n            fail.

## assistant
The alignment convention works in **peak-relative space** and the `_centre_burst` roll is applied in place with no recorded provenance — so absolute time can't be reconstructed from stored fit outputs directly. That's decisive for the design: the switch has to be built as a **scatter correction relative to the profile peak on the fit's own axis**, which is anchor-free and rides on top of the existing absolute anchor. Let me confirm which tests guard the golden JSON.

Critical finding: in the canonical fit, `t0_C = 28.16 ms` and `t0_D = 0.499 ms` live on **independent per-band axes**, and the sharedzeta family doesn't sample `gamma`/`c0` at all — per-channel gains are OLS-recovered against the real cube (`gain_recovered=True`). So the faithful frequency-integrated peak requires reconstructing the model on the actual data, exactly as `dump_jointmodel.recover()` does. Let me survey all 12 canonical fit schemas so I build one reconstructor that covers every family.

These contain the model reconstructed on the true fit axis — exactly what I need to measure the scattered-profile peak. But `t0` (the intrinsic centroid) lives in the fit JSON on that same axis. Let me load one and verify the axes are consistent, then build the correction.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'EOF'\nimport numpy as np, json\nNPZ=\"/Users/jakobfaber/Data/Faber2026/dsa110/flits-runs/data/joint/chromatica_jointmodel_sharedzeta_pbf-exp-exp.npz\"\nz=np.load(NPZ)\nprint(\"NPZ keys:\", list(z.keys()))\nfor k in ['timeC','timeD','freqC','freqD']:\n    a=z[k]; print(f\"  {k}: shape={a.shape} min={a.min():.4f} max={a.max():.4f} dt/df={np.diff(a)[0]:.5f}\")\nprint(\"  modelC shape:\", z['modelC'].shape, \" dataC shape:\", z['dataC'].shape)\nprint(\"  alpha,beta,tau:\", float(z['alpha']), float(z['beta']), float(z['tau_1ghz']))\n# frequency-integrated model profile peak vs t0\nJ=json.load(open(\"analysis/scattering-refit-2026-06/_a1_fits/chromatica_joint_fit_sharedzeta_pbf-exp-exp.json\"))\np={k:v['median'] for k,v in J['percentiles'].items()}\nt0C, t0D = p['t0_C'], p['t0_D']\nfor band,tt,tkey in [('C','timeC',t0C),('D','timeD',t0D)]:\n    t=z[tt]; mod=z[f'model{band}']; data=z[f'data{band}']\n    prof_mod = np.nansum(mod,axis=0)\n    prof_dat = np.nansum(data,axis=0)\n    tpk_mod = t[np.argmax(prof_mod)]\n    tpk_dat = t[np.argmax(prof_dat)]\n    print(f\"band {band}: t0={tkey:.4f}  model_peak={tpk_mod:.4f}  data_peak={tpk_dat:.4f}  (model_peak - t0)={tpk_mod-tkey:+.4f} ms\")\nEOF", "human_description": "Verifying NPZ axes and peak-vs-t0 for chromatica", "environment": "flits-dev"}
> ```

## assistant
NPZ lacks `beta` (older dump) but axes are consistent with the JSON `t0` values. Let me fix and compute the peak-vs-t0 displacement.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'EOF'\nimport numpy as np, json\nNPZ=\"/Users/jakobfaber/Data/Faber2026/dsa110/flits-runs/data/joint/chromatica_jointmodel_sharedzeta_pbf-exp-exp.npz\"\nz=np.load(NPZ)\nJ=json.load(open(\"analysis/scattering-refit-2026-06/_a1_fits/chromatica_joint_fit_sharedzeta_pbf-exp-exp.json\"))\np={k:v['median'] for k,v in J['percentiles'].items()}\nprint(f\"alpha={float(z['alpha']):.4f} tau_1ghz={float(z['tau_1ghz']):.4f} chi2C={float(z['chi2C']):.2f} chi2D={float(z['chi2D']):.2f}\")\nfor band,tkey in [('C','t0_C'),('D','t0_D')]:\n    t=z[f'time{band}']; mod=z[f'model{band}']; data=z[f'data{band}']; t0=p[tkey]\n    dt=np.diff(t)[0]\n    prof_mod=np.nansum(mod,axis=0); prof_dat=np.nansum(data,axis=0)\n    # subsample peak via parabolic interp on model profile\n    im=int(np.argmax(prof_mod))\n    def parpeak(y,i):\n        if i<=0 or i>=len(y)-1: return float(i)\n        d=(y[i-1]-y[i+1]); dd=(y[i-1]-2*y[i]+y[i+1])\n        return i + 0.5*d/dd if dd!=0 else float(i)\n    tpk_mod = t[0]+parpeak(prof_mod,im)*dt\n    tpk_dat = t[np.argmax(prof_dat)]\n    corr = t0 - tpk_mod\n    print(f\"band {band}: t0={t0:.4f}  model_peak={tpk_mod:.4f}  data_peak={tpk_dat:.4f}  scatter_corr(t0-peak)={corr:+.4f} ms  (dt={dt:.4f})\")\nEOF", "human_description": "Computing peak-vs-t0 displacement chromatica", "environment": "flits-dev"}
> ```

## assistant
This is the physics working exactly as predicted. For chromatica:
- **CHIME**: model peak is +0.474 ms later than intrinsic t0 → correction −0.474 ms
- **DSA**: model peak is +0.135 ms later → correction −0.135 ms
- **Differential (C−D) = −0.339 ms** — a large shift on a ~ms-scale offset

And the model peak reproduces the *data* peak to within a sample on both bands (28.635 vs 28.631 ms; DSA within a third of a coarse sample), confirming the current peak-bin pipeline is effectively measuring the scattered-model peak — so `t0 − peak` is exactly the scattering bias to remove.

Rebuild reproduces the NPZ axis exactly (dt=0.06144, len=895, data_peak=28.631). The reconstruction is self-consistent. But the numbers surface the definitional fork I flagged earlier — the bare `t0` parameter (28.161), the frequency-collapsed intrinsic *centroid* (28.309), and the collapsed intrinsic *peak* differ because of the within-band residual-DM tilt. Let me quantify all candidates on both bands before locking the definition.

## assistant
This resolves the definitional fork decisively. The correction must be measured **like-with-like on the same frequency-collapsed axis**, and this is why the bare `t0` parameter is the wrong endpoint:

- The `t0` parameter is referenced at **band-top** frequency (`mu=t0` at `freq.max`), whereas both the scattered peak and the downstream `compute_toa` referral operate at **band-center** `f_center`. Using `t0` directly would inject a band-top-vs-band-center dispersion mismatch.
- The frequency-collapsed **intrinsic centroid** (tau→0) and the collapsed **scattered peak** share the *same* residual-DM tilt and intra-channel smearing, so their difference cancels those and isolates **purely the scattering (PBF) delay** — which is exactly the systematic to remove.

So the operating definition, honoring your "intrinsic centroid t0" choice:
- **Model ToA = frequency-collapsed intrinsic-model centroid** (τ→0, flux-weighted first moment)
- **Δ_scat(band) = scattered-model collapsed peak − intrinsic centroid** (the scattering delay to subtract from the peak-based ToA)
- **Localization error = posterior width of the fitted t0**

For chromatica: Δ_scat(CHIME)=+0.361 ms, Δ_scat(DSA)=+0.009 ms → **differential shift to the offset = −0.352 ms** (large on a ~ms offset). Physics confirmed: CHIME's scattering delay dominates, and the model reproduces the data peak to 0.04 ms.

Let me record this and mark Phase 0 complete.
> **tool_use** `write_memory`
> ```json
> {"human_description": "Recording ToA model-t0 definition decision", "append": [{"text": "ToA rework (2026-07 session): switching FRB ToA from profile peak-bin to joint-fit model. User chose 'intrinsic centroid t0'. Operating definition (validated on chromatica): Model ToA = frequency-collapsed INTRINSIC-model centroid (tau->0 flux-weighted first moment); scatter correction Delta_scat(band) = scattered-model collapsed PEAK - intrinsic centroid; corrected ToA = peakToA - Delta_scat. Localization error = posterior width of fitted t0. Do NOT use the bare t0 PARAMETER: it is referenced at band-TOP freq while scat_peak and downstream compute_toa use band-CENTER f_center; using it injects a dispersion mismatch and fails to cancel the delta_dm tilt. Collapsed intrinsic-vs-scattered on the same axis cancels tilt+smearing and isolates pure PBF scattering delay.", "evidence": "observed"}, {"text": "ToA reconstruction recipe: canonical fits are in analysis/scattering-refit-2026-06/_a1_fits/<burst>_joint_fit<sfx>.json (sharedzeta: casey/chromatica/freya/wilhelm; C1D1 mahi; C3D3 phineas; C2D1 oran/isha/johndoeII [C2D2 RETIRED - CANON map in batch_jointmodel.py is stale]; zach _pbf-exp-exp base; hamilton/whitney use alpha-era joint_json base fit, no beta_C). Rebuild model on true fit axis via BurstDataset using local run configs at ~/Data/Faber2026/dsa110/flits-runs/configs/<burst>_<band>_run.yaml (repoint 'path' to real cube ~/Data/Faber2026/dsa110/DSA_bursts/<burst>_<band>_I_*_cntr_bpc.npy). Per-channel gains OLS-recovered against data exactly like analysis/scattering-refit-2026-06/joint_ppc_multi.py. > {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\n# patch toa_recon: derive beta from alpha when beta_C absent (alpha-era hamilton/whitney)\nimport re\nf=\"/tmp/toa_recon.py\"\nsrc=open(f).read()\n# add beta derivation helper + use in band_correction/make_params\npatch='''\ndef _beta_for(d, band):\n    b = d.get(f\"beta_{band}\", d.get(\"beta_C\"))\n    if b is not None:\n        return float(b)\n    # alpha-era: derive thin-screen beta from alpha (alpha = 2 beta/(beta-2))\n    al = float(d[\"alpha\"][\"median\"])\n    if al <= 2.0 or al >= 100:\n        return 3.6666666666666665\n    beta = 2.0*al/(al-2.0)\n    return float(min(max(beta, 2.01), 4.0))\n'''\nsrc = src.replace(\"def band_params(\", patch + \"\\ndef band_params(\", 1)\nsrc = src.replace('beta = d.get(f\"beta_{band}\", d.get(\"beta_C\", 3.6666666666666665))',\n                  'beta = _beta_for(d, band)')\nopen(f,\"w\").write(src)\nopen(\"/Users/jakobfaber/handoffs/toa_model/toa_recon.py\",\"w\").write(src)\nprint(\"patched _beta_for into toa_recon.py; occurrences of _beta_for:\", src.count(\"_beta_for\"))\nPYEOF", "human_description": "Patching engine for alpha-era beta derivation", "environment": "flits-dev"}
> ```

Several bursts are wrong: mahi/isha/oran show the model peak displaced 4–8 ms from the data peak (for mahi n=1 that's impossible if the axis matched the fit), and hamilton/whitney collapsed to zero (alpha-era base fits, different reconstruction). The likely cause: my `BurstDataset` rebuild with `onpulse_crop=True` regenerated a *different* crop than the fit actually used. The NPZ dumps ARE the true fit axis. Let me diagnose against the NPZ.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nimport numpy as np, glob, os\nND=\"/Users/jakobfaber/Data/Faber2026/dsa110/flits-runs/data/joint\"\nimport sys; sys.path.insert(0,\"/tmp\")\nimport toa_recon as R\nfor b in [\"mahi\",\"isha\",\"chromatica\"]:\n    # find NPZ\n    hits=glob.glob(f\"{ND}/{b}_jointmodel*pbf-exp-exp.npz\")\n    if not hits: print(b,\"no npz\"); continue\n    z=np.load(hits[0])\n    m=R.prepare(b,\"chime\")\n    tnpz=z['timeC']; trec=np.asarray(m.time,float)\n    print(f\"{b}: NPZ axis len={len(tnpz)} dt={np.diff(tnpz)[0]:.5f} span=[{tnpz[0]:.3f},{tnpz[-1]:.3f}]  data_peak={tnpz[np.argmax(np.nansum(z['dataC'],0))]:.3f}\")\n    print(f\"{'':{len(b)+2}}REBUILT len={len(trec)} dt={np.diff(trec)[0]:.5f} span=[{trec[0]:.3f},{trec[-1]:.3f}]  data_peak={trec[np.argmax(np.nansum(m.data,0))]:.3f}\")\n    print(f\"{'':{len(b)+2}}NPZ model_peak={tnpz[np.argmax(np.nansum(z['modelC'],0))]:.3f}\")\nPYEOF", "human_description": "Comparing rebuilt axis vs NPZ for mahi isha chromatica", "environment": "flits-dev"}
> ```

## assistant
The rebuild matches the NPZ axis exactly — so this isn't a rebuild bug. But the *stored* NPZ model's collapsed peak is also wrong for mahi (peaks at t=0) and isha (4 ms off), while chromatica is perfect. The cause: the per-channel OLS gain recovery is unconstrained, so in low-SNR channels it fits noise and produces edge spikes that dominate a naive frequency sum. Let me diagnose mahi and test a robust collapse (valid-mask + clip unphysical negative gains + noise weighting).
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nimport numpy as np, glob\nND=\"/Users/jakobfaber/Data/Faber2026/dsa110/flits-runs/data/joint\"\nz=np.load(glob.glob(f\"{ND}/mahi_jointmodel*pbf-exp-exp.npz\")[0])\nmod=z['modelC']; dat=z['dataC']; t=z['timeC']; noise=z['noiseC']; valid=z['validC']\nprint(\"modelC shape\",mod.shape,\"valid shape\",valid.shape, \"valid sum\", np.asarray(valid).sum() if valid.ndim==1 else valid.shape)\n# naive collapse\nprof=np.nansum(mod,0)\nprint(\"naive collapse argmax t=\",t[np.argmax(prof)], \" max=\",prof.max())\n# which channel dominates at t=0?\nprint(\"model[:,0] (t=0 col) top channels:\", np.argsort(mod[:,0])[-5:], \"vals\", np.sort(mod[:,0])[-5:])\nprint(\"per-channel max over time (first few):\", mod.max(1)[:8])\n# valid-mask collapse\nv=np.asarray(valid)\nif v.ndim==1:\n    profv=np.nansum(mod[v],0); print(\"valid-mask collapse argmax t=\",t[np.argmax(profv)])\n# noise-weighted (1/noise^2) collapse of model\nw=1.0/np.clip(noise,1e-9,None)**2\nprofw=np.nansum(mod*w[:,None],0); print(\"noise-weighted collapse argmax t=\",t[np.argmax(profw)])\n# data peak for ref\nprint(\"data collapse argmax t=\",t[np.argmax(np.nansum(dat,0))])\n# data noise-weighted\nprofdw=np.nansum(dat*w[:,None],0); print(\"data noise-weighted argmax t=\",t[np.argmax(profdw)])\nPYEOF", "human_description": "Diagnosing mahi collapse artifact", "environment": "flits-dev"}
> ```

## assistant
The mahi NPZ model is nearly flat (~0.004 everywhere) — a gain-recovery failure in that stored dump, not a real model. Let me bypass the OLS-gain collapse entirely: the scattering delay is deterministic per channel from the model kernel itself. Let me check where the raw model *kernel* (unit gain) peaks vs the fitted t0, which is gain-independent.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nimport sys; sys.path.insert(0,\"/tmp\")\nimport numpy as np, json\nfrom dataclasses import replace\nimport importlib, toa_recon as R; importlib.reload(R)\n\nfor b in [\"mahi\",\"isha\",\"chromatica\"]:\n    d=json.load(open(R.fit_path(b))); p={k:v['median'] for k,v in d['percentiles'].items()}\n    m=R.prepare(b,\"chime\"); freq=np.asarray(m.freq,float); t=np.asarray(m.time,float); dt=float(np.diff(t)[0])\n    tau=p['tau_1ghz']; beta=R._beta_for(d,'C'); n=int(d.get('components_C',1))\n    if d.get('shared_zeta'):\n        z=p['zeta_1ghz']*freq**p['x_zeta']\n        pscat=[R.FRBParams(c0=1.,t0=p['t0_C'],gamma=0.,zeta=z,tau_1ghz=tau,beta=beta,delta_dm=p['delta_dm_C'])]\n    else:\n        pscat=R.band_params(p,'C',n,tau,beta)\n    pintr=[replace(pp,tau_1ghz=0.0) for pp in pscat]\n    # UNIT-GAIN kernel collapse (equal weight per channel, model's own amp via c0=1,gamma=0 => flat spectrum)\n    Ks=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pscat]); Ki=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pintr])\n    prof_scat=Ks.sum(0).sum(0); prof_intr=Ki.sum(0).sum(0)\n    def pk(y): i=int(np.argmax(y)); return t[0]+R.parpeak(y,i)*dt\n    print(f\"{b}: unit-gain scat_peak={pk(prof_scat):.3f} intr_peak={pk(prof_intr):.3f} intr_cent={R.first_moment(t,prof_intr):.3f}  t0_C={p.get('t0_C',[p.get('t0_C1')]):}\")\n    print(f\"    data_peak={t[np.argmax(np.nansum(m.data,0))]:.3f}  n_comp={n}\")\nPYEOF", "human_description": "Testing unit-gain kernel collapse mahi isha", "environment": "flits-dev"}
> ```

I need to audit axis-consistency across all 12 before trusting any correction.

> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nimport sys; sys.path.insert(0,\"/tmp\")\nimport numpy as np, json, importlib, toa_recon as R; importlib.reload(R)\nBURSTS=[\"casey\",\"chromatica\",\"freya\",\"wilhelm\",\"mahi\",\"phineas\",\"oran\",\"isha\",\"johndoeII\",\"zach\",\"hamilton\",\"whitney\"]\nprint(f\"{'burst':12s} {'band':5s} {'t0(min..max of comps)':22s} {'axis_span':16s} {'data_pk':8s} {'t0_in_axis?':10s}\")\nfor b in BURSTS:\n    d=json.load(open(R.fit_path(b))); p={k:v['median'] for k,v in d['percentiles'].items()}\n    for band,tel in [(\"C\",\"chime\"),(\"D\",\"dsa\")]:\n        try:\n            m=R.prepare(b,tel); t=np.asarray(m.time,float)\n            n=int(d.get(f'components_{band}',1))\n            if d.get('shared_zeta') or f't0_{band}' in p:\n                t0s=[p.get(f't0_{band}')]\n            else:\n                t0s=[p.get(f't0_{band}{i}') for i in range(1,n+1)]\n            t0s=[x for x in t0s if x is not None]\n            dp=t[np.argmax(np.nansum(m.data,0))]\n            inax=all(t[0]-1<=x<=t[-1]+1 for x in t0s)\n            print(f\"{b:12s} {tel:5s} [{min(t0s):8.2f}..{max(t0s):8.2f}]      [{t[0]:.1f}..{t[-1]:6.1f}]   {dp:8.2f} {'YES' if inax else 'NO <<<':10s}\")\n        except Exception as e:\n            print(f\"{b:12s} {tel:5s} ERR {type(e).__name__}: {str(e)[:40]}\")\nPYEOF", "human_description": "Auditing t0 axis-consistency across 12 bursts", "environment": "flits-dev"}
> ```

run: FLITS_REPO={REPO} FLITS_RUNS={runs} \"\n            f\"python {HERE / 'dump_jointmodel.py'} {burst} {suffix}\"\n        )\n\n    z = np.load(fp, allow_pickle=True)\n    bands = _aligned_bands(z, burst)\n    fig = plot_codetection(\n        bands,\n        columns=(\"data\", \"model\", \"resid\"),\n        show_model_on_data=False,\n        per_band_scale=True,\n        gap_label=False,\n        figsize=(12.4, 4.9),\n        band_labels=False,\n        show_column_titles=False,\n        per_b

## assistant
zach uses a bespoke `cwin` window — its axis can't be rebuilt from the standard config, but its NPZ dump has the correct axis and data. This points to a cleaner, uniform engine: **source the time/freq axes and data directly from each burst's authoritative NPZ dump**, build `FRBModel` on those axes (supplying native `df_MHz`/`dm_init` from the telescope config), OLS-recover the component gains against the **data** (the fit's own procedure), then evaluate scattered vs τ→0 intrinsic with those gains. This sidesteps BurstDataset entirely and works for zach and the corrupt-NPZ bursts alike. Let me build and validate it.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\ncat > /tmp/toa_recon2.py <<'PYEOF'\n\"\"\"Uniform model-ToA scatter correction sourced from authoritative NPZ dumps.\n\nFor each burst/band: take the NPZ's data + time/freq axes (the true fit axis),\nbuild FRBModel on those axes with native df_MHz + dm_init, OLS-recover component\ngains against the DATA (the fit's own gain recovery, per joint_ppc_multi),\nevaluate scattered (tau=fit) and intrinsic (tau->0) collapsed profiles with the\nSAME gains. ## assistant
chromatica broke under the NPZ approach: peak reproduces data to only 0.19 ms (was 0.04 via BurstDataset), and the correction flipped sign. The clue is in the numbers — chromatica's sharedzeta fit reports **fitted α=3.28**, but I'm forcing β=3.667 (→ α_from_β=4.4). α=3.28 < 4 is impossible under the thin-screen β↔α map, so these sharedzeta fits must scale τ∝ν^−α with **α as a free parameter**, not via β. My reconstruction is using the wrong τ(ν) law. Let me find how the fitter handles free α.

## assistant
The model is beta-native: `τ = tau_1ghz · ν^(−α)` with `α = alpha_from_beta(β)`, and for an **"exp" PBF the kernel uses β=4 (α=4)** regardless of the stored `beta_C=3.667` placeholder. The reported α=3.28 is a diagnostic, not the kernel value. My reconstruction used β=3.667 → wrong τ(ν). Let me key β off `pbf_C`/`pbf_D` and re-validate.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\n# patch _beta_for in toa_recon2: exp PBF -> beta=4.0 (alpha=4); else beta_C; else derive\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nf=\"/tmp/toa_recon2.py\"; s=open(f).read()\nold='''def _beta_for(d, band):\n    b=d.get(f\"beta_{band}\", d.get(\"beta_C\"))\n    if b is not None: return float(b)\n    al=float(d[\"alpha\"][\"median\"])\n    if al<=2.0 or al>=100: return 3.6666666666666665\n    return float(min(max(2.0*al/(al-2.0),2.01),4.0))'''\nnew='''def _beta_for(d, band):\n    # PBF family drives beta: an exponential PBF is uniquely beta=4 (alpha=4).\n    pbf=d.get(f\"pbf_{band}\", d.get(\"pbf_C\"))\n    if pbf==\"exp\":\n        return 4.0\n    b=d.get(f\"beta_{band}\", d.get(\"beta_C\"))\n    if b is not None: return float(b)\n    al=float(d[\"alpha\"][\"median\"])\n    if al<=2.0 or al>=100: return 3.6666666666666665\n    return float(min(max(2.0*al/(al-2.0),2.01),4.0))'''\nassert old in s; s=s.replace(old,new); open(f,\"w\").write(s)\nopen(\"/Users/jakobfaber/handoffs/toa_model/toa_recon2.py\",\"w\").write(s)\nprint(\"patched _beta_for: exp PBF -> beta=4\")\nPYEOF\necho \"=== re-validate all 12 both bands: peak_reproduces_data must be small ===\"\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nimport sys; ## assistant
Now I see the structure clearly: for the 8 single-component bursts my reconstruction matches the authoritative NPZ model peak to ~1 sample (0.05–0.09 ms), confirming fidelity. The multi-component MARGINAL fits (oran, phineas, isha) differ by ~0.5 ms because my OLS gains (recovered against data) differ from the published dump's gains. 

The fix: recover gains by projecting onto the **NPZ's stored model** rather than the data. That makes my scattered reconstruction identical to the authoritative published model by construction, and the same matched gains then define the intrinsic (τ→0) profile. Let me implement and validate.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\n# Patch band_result: recover gains by OLS onto NPZ stored model (authoritative),\n# so scattered_recon == NPZ model; use matched gains for tau->0 intrinsic.\nf=\"/tmp/toa_recon2.py\"; s=open(f).read()\nold='''    Ks=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pscat])   # (N,F,T)\n    Ki=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pintr])\n    # OLS gains per channel vs DATA\n    M=np.einsum(\"nft,mft->fnm\",Ks,Ks); b=np.einsum(\"nft,ft->fn\",Ks,data)\n    N=len(pscat); jit=1e-9*max(float(np.einsum(\"fnn->f\",M).mean()),1e-30)\n    g=np.linalg.solve(M+jit*np.eye(N),b[...,None])[...,0]\n    mod_scat=np.einsum(\"fn,nft->ft\",g,Ks); mod_intr=np.einsum(\"fn,nft->ft\",g,Ki)'''\nnew='''    Ks=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pscat])   # (N,F,T)\n    Ki=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pintr])\n    npz_model=np.asarray(z[f\"model{band}\"],float)\n    # Recover gains by OLS onto the AUTHORITATIVE stored model, so the scattered\n    # reconstruction reproduces the published dump exactly; matched gains then\n    # define the tau->0 intrinsic profile. Fall back to data if the stored model\n    # is flat/corrupt (chromatica DSA).\n    prof_npz=np.nansum(npz_model,0)\n    target = data if (prof_npz.max()-prof_npz.min())/(abs(prof_npz.max())+1e-30) < 0.05 else npz_model\n    M=np.einsum(\"nft,mft->fnm\",Ks,Ks); b=np.einsum(\"nft,ft->fn\",Ks,target)\n    N=len(pscat); jit=1e-9*max(float(np.einsum(\"fnn->f\",M).mean()),1e-30)\n    g=np.linalg.solve(M+jit*np.eye(N),b[...,None])[...,0]\n    mod_scat=np.einsum(\"fn,nft->ft\",g,Ks); mod_intr=np.einsum(\"fn,nft->ft\",g,Ki)'''\nassert old in s; s=s.replace(old,new); open(f,\"w\").write(s)\nopen(\"/Users/jakobfaber/handoffs/toa_model/toa_recon2.py\",\"w\").write(s)\nprint(\"patched: gains recovered onto authoritative NPZ model\")\nPYEOF\necho \"=== r
> ```

## assistant
chromatica CHIME regressed (30.4 vs NPZ 28.9) because forcing β=4 over-steepens τ(ν) for that FAIL-verdict fit. The β/α question is fragile when read from the JSON. But the NPZ dumps store the **actual** `alpha`/`beta`/`tau_1ghz` used to generate the published model. Let me source those directly from the NPZ and, decisively, fit β to reproduce the stored model.

So **the NPZ's stored `beta`/`tau_1ghz` are the true kernel values** — and for chromatica the JSON I was reading (α=3.28) is a *different* fit than the one that made the NPZ (α=4.0). I'll source β and τ from the NPZ, keep t0/ζ/δDM from the fit JSON, and gate on projection residual.

> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nf=\"/tmp/toa_recon2.py\"; s=open(f).read()\n# 1) source tau & beta from NPZ; keep t0/zeta/delta_dm/n from JSON\nold_params='''    pscat,tau,beta,n=_params(d,p,band,freq)'''\nnew_params='''    tau_npz=float(z[\"tau_1ghz\"]) if \"tau_1ghz\" in z else None\n    beta_npz=float(z[\"beta\"]) if \"beta\" in z else None\n    pscat,tau,beta,n=_params(d,p,band,freq,tau_override=tau_npz,beta_override=beta_npz)'''\nassert old_params in s; s=s.replace(old_params,new_params)\n# 2) extend _params signature\nold_sig='''def _params(d, p, band, freq):\n    tau=p[\"tau_1ghz\"]; beta=_beta_for(d,band); n=int(d.get(f\"components_{band}\",1))'''\nnew_sig='''def _params(d, p, band, freq, tau_override=None, beta_override=None):\n    tau=tau_override if tau_override is not None else p[\"tau_1ghz\"]\n    beta=beta_override if beta_override is not None else _beta_for(d,band)\n    n=int(d.get(f\"components_{band}\",1))'''\nassert old_sig in s; s=s.replace(old_sig,new_sig)\n# 3) rebuild FRBModel with beta AFTER we know it -> move model build after _params.\n# Currently model m built before _params. > ```

> **tool_result**: {"stdout":"def band_result(burstkey, band):\n    tel = \"chime\" if band==\"C\" else \"dsa\"\n    fit,npz,vb=sources(burstkey)\n    d=json.load(open(fit)); p={k:v[\"median\"] for k,v in d[\"percentiles\"].items()}\n    perr={k:(abs(v.get(\"err_minus\",0.))+abs(v.get(\"err_plus\",0.)))/2 for k,v in d[\"percentiles\"].items()}\n    z=np.load(npz)\n    t=np.asarray(z[f\"time{band}\"],float); freq=np.asarray(z[f\"freq{band}\"],float)\n    data=np.asarray(z[f\"data{band}\"],float); noise=np.asarray(z[f\"noise{band}\"],float)\n    dt=float(np.diff(t)[0])\n    beta=_beta_for(d,band)\n    m=FRBModel(t, freq, data=data, dm_init=_cfg_dm_init(burstkey,tel),\n               df_MHz=NATIVE_DF[tel], beta=beta, noise_std=noise)\n    pscat,tau,beta,n=_params(d,p,band,freq)\n    pintr=[replace(pp,tau_1ghz=0.0) for pp in pscat]\n    Ks=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pscat])   # (N,F,T)\n    Ki=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pintr])\n    npz_model=np.asarray(z[f\"model{band}\"],float)\n    # Recover gains by OLS onto the AUTHORITATIVE stored model, so the scattered\n    # reconstruction reproduces the published dump exactly; matched gains then\n    # define the tau->0 intrinsic profile. Fall back to data if the stored model\n    # is flat/corrupt (chromatica DSA).\n    prof_npz=np.nansum(npz_model,0)\n    target = data if (prof_npz.max()-prof_npz.min())/(abs(prof_npz.max())+1e-30) < 0.05 else npz_model\n    M=np.einsum(\"nft,mft->fnm\",Ks,Ks); b=np.einsum(\"nft,ft->fn\",Ks,target)\n    N=len(pscat); jit=1e-9*max(float(np.einsum(\"fnn->f\",M).mean()),1e-30)\n    g=np.linalg.solve(M+jit*np.eye(N),b[...,None])[...,0]\n    mod_scat=np.einsum(\"fn,nft->ft\",g,Ks); mod_intr=np.einsum(\"fn,nft->ft\",g,Ki)\n    prof_scat=np.nansum(mod_scat,0); prof_intr=np.nansum(mod_intr,0); prof_dat=np.nansum(data,0)\n    scat_peak=t[0]+parpeak(prof_scat,int(np.argmax(prof_scat)))*dt\n    data_peak=t[int(np.argmax(prof_dat))]\n    arrival=first_moment(t,pro

## assistant
The proj_resid on CHIME bands is high even where the collapsed peak matches to a sample, and the multi-component intrinsic centroids (oran −10 ms, phineas +5.5 ms) are clearly nonphysical. The instability is in applying scattered-model OLS gains (which can go negative to handle component overlap) to the τ→0 intrinsic kernels, then taking a first moment. Let me debug oran directly.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nimport sys; sys.path.insert(0,\"/tmp\"); import numpy as np, json\nfrom dataclasses import replace\nimport toa_recon2 as R2\nb=\"oran\"; band=\"C\"\nfit,npz,vb=R2.sources(b); d=json.load(open(fit)); p={k:v[\"median\"] for k,v in d[\"percentiles\"].items()}\nz=np.load(npz); t=np.asarray(z['timeC'],float); freq=np.asarray(z['freqC'],float); data=z['dataC']; noise=z['noiseC']\ntau=float(z['tau_1ghz']); beta=float(z['beta'])\nm=R2.FRBModel(t,freq,data=data,dm_init=0.0,df_MHz=R2.NATIVE_DF['chime'],beta=beta,noise_std=noise)\npscat,tau,beta,n=R2._params(d,p,'C',freq,tau_override=tau,beta_override=beta)\nprint(\"oran C t0s:\", [round(pp.t0,3) for pp in pscat], \"zetas:\", [round(float(np.mean(pp.zeta)),3) for pp in pscat])\nKs=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pscat])\nnpzm=z['modelC']\nM=np.einsum(\"nft,mft->fnm\",Ks,Ks); bb=np.einsum(\"nft,ft->fn\",Ks,npzm)\nN=len(pscat); jit=1e-9*max(float(np.einsum(\"fnn->f\",M).mean()),1e-30)\ng=np.linalg.solve(M+jit*np.eye(N),bb[...,None])[...,0]\nprint(\"gain per comp: sum over freq:\", g.sum(0), \" min:\", g.min(0), \" max:\", g.max(0))\nprint(\"negative gain fraction per comp:\", [(g[:,i]<0).mean() for i in range(N)])\n# component-integrated positive flux weights\nKi=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.,tau_1ghz=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pscat])\nmod_intr=np.einsum(\"fn,nft->ft\",g,Ki); prof_intr=np.nansum(mod_intr,0)\nprint(\"intr profile: min\",prof_intr.min(),\"max\",prof_intr.max(),\"argmax t=\",t[np.argmax(prof_intr)])\n# per-component intrinsic collapsed peak\nfor i in range(N):\n    pi=np.nansum(g[:,i][:,None]*Ki[i],0)\n    print(f\"  comp{i}: t0={pscat[i].t0:.3f} weighted_peak_t={t[np.argmax(pi)]:.3f} totflux={pi.sum():.3f}\")\nPYEOF", "human_description": "Debugging oran multi-component intrinsic instability", "environment": "flits-dev"}
> ```

## assistant
Root cause found: oran's two components (t0 = 13.13, 14.73 ms) are separated by only 1.6 ms with τ = 0.84 ms scattering, so the two scattered kernels are nearly collinear — the per-channel OLS gains explode to +179/−159 with opposite signs. That decomposition is fine for reproducing the *scattered* model (by construction) but is not identifiable for the intrinsic profile.

The fix is to eliminate the per-component decomposition entirely. Because convolution is linear, the centroid shift from scattering equals the **mean of the PBF**, independent of components, gains, or spectrum: `centroid(x ⊗ PBF) = centroid(x) + centroid(PBF)`. So I can compute the intrinsic centroid from the authoritative stored model directly.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\n# Prototype the decomposition-free scatter correction:\n#   intrinsic_centroid_collapsed = scattered_centroid_collapsed - <PBF_mean>_fluxweighted\n#   scatter_correction = intrinsic_centroid - scattered_peak\n# PBF_mean(f) computed as centroid(K_scat[f]) - centroid(K_intr[f]) for a single\n# representative unit kernel (gain/spectrum-independent). Flux weight from stored model.\nimport sys; sys.path.insert(0,\"/tmp\"); import numpy as np, json\nfrom dataclasses import replace\nimport toa_recon2 as R2\n\ndef correction_v4(b, band):\n    fit,npz,vb=R2.sources(b); d=json.load(open(fit)); p={k:v[\"median\"] for k,v in d[\"percentiles\"].items()}\n    tel=\"chime\" if band==\"C\" else \"dsa\"\n    z=np.load(npz); t=np.asarray(z[f'time{band}'],float); freq=np.asarray(z[f'freq{band}'],float)\n    npzm=np.asarray(z[f'model{band}'],float); noise=z[f'noise{band}']; dt=float(np.diff(t)[0])\n    tau=float(z['tau_1ghz']); beta=float(z['beta'])\n    m=R2.FRBModel(t,freq,data=npzm,dm_init=R2._cfg_dm_init(b,tel),df_MHz=R2.NATIVE_DF[tel],beta=beta,noise_std=noise)\n    # representative single kernel at the (mean) t0/zeta of the fit (gain-independent centroid shift)\n    pscat,_,_,n=R2._params(d,p,band,freq,tau_override=tau,beta_override=beta)\n    # per-channel PBF mean = centroid(K_scat[f]) - centroid(K_intr[f]) averaged over components\n    def chan_centroids(par):\n        K=m(replace(par,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\")  # (F,T)\n        w=np.clip(K,0,None); s=w.sum(1); s[s==0]=1\n        return (K*t[None,:]).sum(1)/np.where(K.sum(1)==0,1,K.sum(1))\n    # use flux-weighted average of per-component scatter delay\n    dscat=np.zeros(len(freq)); wtot=0.0\n    for par in pscat:\n        cs=chan_centroids(replace(par,tau_1ghz=tau)); ci=chan_centroids(replace(par,tau_1ghz=0.0))\n        dscat += (cs-ci); wtot+=1\n    dscat/=wtot  # per-channel PBF mean (ms)\n    # flux weight f
> ```

## assistant
This is stable now — no more ±10 ms blowups. But there's an internal inconsistency I need to resolve: for chromatica the PBF-subtraction gives intrinsic centroid 25.19 ms, yet the fit's own `t0_C` is ~28.16 ms. These should agree. The culprit is tail-truncation in my per-channel PBF-mean estimate (chromatica's band-bottom τ reaches ~8 ms, longer than the model's evaluated window). Let me cross-check against the parameter-direct definition, which is immune to tail truncation.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\n# Parameter-direct intrinsic centroid: flux-weighted mean of component mu(f)=t0_c+dd(f),\n# with component flux weights from NNLS onto the stored model (stable, non-negative).\nimport sys; sys.path.insert(0,\"/tmp\"); import numpy as np, json\nfrom dataclasses import replace\nfrom scipy.optimize import nnls\nimport toa_recon2 as R2\n\nDM_DELAY=4.148808\ndef dd(freq, delta_dm, beta, fmax):\n    return DM_DELAY*delta_dm*(freq**-beta - fmax**-beta)\n\ndef direct(b, band):\n    fit,npz,vb=R2.sources(b); d=json.load(open(fit)); p={k:v[\"median\"] for k,v in d[\"percentiles\"].items()}\n    tel=\"chime\" if band==\"C\" else \"dsa\"\n    z=np.load(npz); t=np.asarray(z[f'time{band}'],float); freq=np.asarray(z[f'freq{band}'],float)\n    npzm=np.asarray(z[f'model{band}'],float); noise=z[f'noise{band}']; dt=float(np.diff(t)[0])\n    tau=float(z['tau_1ghz']); beta=float(z['beta']); fmax=freq.max(); fcen=float(np.median(freq))\n    m=R2.FRBModel(t,freq,data=npzm,dm_init=R2._cfg_dm_init(b,tel),df_MHz=R2.NATIVE_DF[tel],beta=beta,noise_std=noise)\n    pscat,_,_,n=R2._params(d,p,band,freq,tau_override=tau,beta_override=beta)\n    ddm=float(p.get(f\"delta_dm_{band}\",0.0))\n    # component t0 list\n    t0s=np.array([pp.t0 for pp in pscat])\n    # NNLS per-channel gains onto stored model -> non-negative component fluxes\n    Ks=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pscat])  # (N,F,T)\n    flux_c=np.zeros(n)\n    for fi in range(len(freq)):\n        A=Ks[:,fi,:].T  # (T,N)\n        y=npzm[fi]\n        try:\n            g,_=nnls(A,y); flux_c+=g*np.array([Ks[j,fi].sum() for j in range(n)])\n        except Exception:\n            pass\n    if flux_c.sum()<=0: flux_c=np.ones(n)\n    w=flux_c/flux_c.sum()\n    arrival_bandtop=float((w*t0s).sum())\n    arrival_fcen = arrival_bandtop + dd(np.array([fcen]),ddm,beta,fmax)[0]\n    prof=np.nansum(npzm,0); ip=in
> ```

## assistant
The single-component bursts are clean, but two problems remain: (1) applying a separate `dd(f_center)` referral double-counts dispersion (the scattered peak already carries it), producing the oran −5 ms artifact; (2) flux-weighted mean of *well-separated* component t0s lands in a gap with no flux (isha's two components are 16 ms apart). 

The correct, stable formulation is the one my very first BurstDataset run used, now with the instability fixed: reconstruct **both** the scattered and intrinsic collapsed profiles on the *same* axis with **NNLS** (non-negative) gains, so dispersion cancels, the intrinsic profile has no tail (centroid is robust), and collinear components can't blow up. Let me implement this as the final engine.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nimport sys; sys.path.insert(0,\"/tmp\"); import numpy as np, json\nfrom dataclasses import replace\nfrom scipy.optimize import nnls\nimport toa_recon2 as R2\n\ndef band_v5(b, band):\n    fit,npz,vb=R2.sources(b); d=json.load(open(fit)); p={k:v[\"median\"] for k,v in d[\"percentiles\"].items()}\n    perr={k:(abs(v.get(\"err_minus\",0.))+abs(v.get(\"err_plus\",0.)))/2 for k,v in d[\"percentiles\"].items()}\n    tel=\"chime\" if band==\"C\" else \"dsa\"\n    z=np.load(npz); t=np.asarray(z[f'time{band}'],float); freq=np.asarray(z[f'freq{band}'],float)\n    npzm=np.asarray(z[f'model{band}'],float); data=np.asarray(z[f'data{band}'],float)\n    noise=z[f'noise{band}']; dt=float(np.diff(t)[0])\n    tau=float(z['tau_1ghz']); beta=float(z['beta'])\n    m=R2.FRBModel(t,freq,data=npzm,dm_init=R2._cfg_dm_init(b,tel),df_MHz=R2.NATIVE_DF[tel],beta=beta,noise_std=noise)\n    pscat,_,_,n=R2._params(d,p,band,freq,tau_override=tau,beta_override=beta)\n    pintr=[replace(pp,tau_1ghz=0.0) for pp in pscat]\n    Ks=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pscat])  # (N,F,T)\n    Ki=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pintr])\n    prof_npz=np.nansum(npzm,0)\n    target = data if (prof_npz.max()-prof_npz.min())/(abs(prof_npz.max())+1e-30)<0.05 else npzm\n    # NNLS per-channel gains onto authoritative target (stable, non-negative)\n    G=np.zeros((len(freq),n))\n    for fi in range(len(freq)):\n        A=Ks[:,fi,:].T; y=np.nan_to_num(target[fi])\n        try: G[fi],_=nnls(A,y)\n        except Exception: G[fi]=0.0\n    mod_scat=np.einsum(\"fn,nft->ft\",G,Ks); mod_intr=np.einsum(\"fn,nft->ft\",G,Ki)\n    resid=float(np.sqrt(np.nansum((mod_scat-target)**2)/(np.nansum(target**2)+1e-30)))\n    ps=np.nansum(mod_scat,0); pI=np.nansum(mod_intr,0); pdat=np.nansum(data,0)\n    scat_peak=t[0]+R2.parpeak(ps,int(np.argmax(ps)))*dt\n    intr_cent=R2.f
> ```

## assistant
NNLS still fails for oran (intrinsic peak piled at t=0). The remaining instability is fundamental: for multi-component bursts with large τ, reconstructing a full intrinsic *profile* and taking its global centroid/peak is ill-posed. But the **per-component intrinsic centroid** is stable — each component's intrinsic kernel is a clean tail-free Gaussian whose centroid is well-defined. Since the pipeline's peak convention tracks the *dominant* feature, the consistent model ToA is the intrinsic centroid of the component that dominates the collapsed scattered peak. Let me implement this.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nimport sys; sys.path.insert(0,\"/tmp\"); import numpy as np, json\nfrom dataclasses import replace\nfrom scipy.optimize import nnls\nimport toa_recon2 as R2\n\ndef band_v6(b, band):\n    fit,npz,vb=R2.sources(b); d=json.load(open(fit)); p={k:v[\"median\"] for k,v in d[\"percentiles\"].items()}\n    perr={k:(abs(v.get(\"err_minus\",0.))+abs(v.get(\"err_plus\",0.)))/2 for k,v in d[\"percentiles\"].items()}\n    tel=\"chime\" if band==\"C\" else \"dsa\"\n    z=np.load(npz); t=np.asarray(z[f'time{band}'],float); freq=np.asarray(z[f'freq{band}'],float)\n    npzm=np.asarray(z[f'model{band}'],float); data=np.asarray(z[f'data{band}'],float)\n    noise=z[f'noise{band}']; dt=float(np.diff(t)[0])\n    tau=float(z['tau_1ghz']); beta=float(z['beta'])\n    m=R2.FRBModel(t,freq,data=npzm,dm_init=R2._cfg_dm_init(b,tel),df_MHz=R2.NATIVE_DF[tel],beta=beta,noise_std=noise)\n    pscat,_,_,n=R2._params(d,p,band,freq,tau_override=tau,beta_override=beta)\n    pintr=[replace(pp,tau_1ghz=0.0) for pp in pscat]\n    Ks=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pscat])\n    Ki=np.stack([m(replace(pp,c0=1.,gamma=0.),\"M3\") for pp in pintr])\n    prof_npz=np.nansum(npzm,0)\n    target = data if (prof_npz.max()-prof_npz.min())/(abs(prof_npz.max())+1e-30)<0.05 else npzm\n    G=np.zeros((len(freq),n))\n    for fi in range(len(freq)):\n        A=Ks[:,fi,:].T; y=np.nan_to_num(target[fi])\n        try: G[fi],_=nnls(A,y)\n        except Exception: G[fi]=0.0\n    # per-component integrated scattered flux -> dominant component\n    Fscat=np.array([ (G[:,j][:,None]*Ks[j]).sum() for j in range(n)])\n    dom=int(np.argmax(Fscat))\n    # per-component intrinsic centroid (clean Gaussians, stable), gain+flux weighted over freq\n    def comp_intr_centroid(j):\n        wf = G[:,j]*np.nansum(Ki[j],1)          # per-channel weight\n        cf = np.array([R2.first_moment(t,Ki[j,fi])
> ```

## assistant
oran CHIME is still wrong (i_cent=6.879 but t0=13.13). The per-channel NNLS-gain weighting is corrupting the dispersion average differently per component. The dispersion offset `<dd(f)>` is component-independent (same δDM law), so I should compute it *once* from channel model-flux and add it to each component's t0. And per-channel centroids should use the parabolic argmax of the clean Gaussian (tail/truncation-immune), not a truncated first moment. Let me fix and validate oran specifically.

## assistant
Now oran is fully diagnosed: δDM_C = −0.287 disperses each component across the *entire* CHIME band (comp0 sweeps from t=13.1 at band-top down to t=0 at band-bottom), so the collapsed intrinsic centroid/peak is genuinely ill-defined — flux piles at the t=0 edge.

The resolution is to cancel dispersion **per-channel before collapsing**, which is exact. For a symmetric intrinsic Gaussian, its centroid = its center = μ(f), so the scattering bias in the measured peak is simply:

**Δ_scat = Σ_f w(f)·[peak(K_scat[f]) − μ(f)]**, flux-weighted

This is component-independent, gain-independent, dispersion-free, and matches the user's "intrinsic centroid" intent exactly. Let me implement it as the final engine and validate.

## assistant
oran is now stable (Δscat=1.64ms, physical for τ=0.84), but two multi-component bursts have unphysical corrections (isha 9.4ms, phineas 6.8ms ≫ τ), and chromatica's reconstruction reproduces the data peak to only +1.8ms with the NPZ β. 

I've been fighting reconstruction fidelity. The decisive simplification: **I don't need to reconstruct the scattered model — the NPZ stores it authoritatively.** I only need the intrinsic (τ→0) arrival, which follows analytically: `centroid(scattered) = centroid(intrinsic) + mean(PBF)`, where mean(PBF at f) is a pure function of τ(f) and β. This uses only the authoritative stored model plus the analytic PBF mean — no gain recovery, no collapse pathology. Let me find the PBF generator to get its mean exactly.

## assistant
The model uses an exponential PBF (β≥3.98) or a power-law thin-screen PBF (β<3.98). The key property: **centroid is linear under convolution**, so `centroid(scattered) = centroid(intrinsic) + mean(PBF)` exactly — while the *peak* shift is not simply τ. So the robust correction is:

**Δ_scat = P_scat − C_scat + ⟨PBF_mean⟩**

where P_scat (collapsed peak) and C_scat (collapsed centroid) come **directly from the authoritative NPZ model** (no reconstruction), and ⟨PBF_mean⟩ is the flux-weighted per-channel centroid shift computed from the model's own convolution functions (gain/component-independent). This eliminates every instability. Let me implement and validate against the chromatica benchmark (Δ_scat_C≈+0.36).
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\ncat > /tmp/toa_recon3.py <<'PYEOF'\n\"\"\"Robust model-ToA scatter correction (v8).\n\nUses ONLY the authoritative stored NPZ scattered model + the analytic PBF mean.\n  Delta_scat = P_scat - C_scat + <PBF_mean>_fluxweighted   [subtract from peak-ToA]\n  intrinsic_centroid_collapsed = C_scat - <PBF_mean>\nwhere P_scat/C_scat are the collapsed peak/centroid of the NPZ model (robust,\nno reconstruction), and <PBF_mean>(f) = centroid(unit scattered kernel) -\ncentroid(unit intrinsic kernel), a pure property of tau(f),beta (gain/component\nindependent since convolution shifts the centroid by the PBF mean regardless of\nthe intrinsic shape).\n\"\"\"\nfrom __future__ import annotations\nimport json, os\nfrom dataclasses import replace\nimport numpy as np, yaml\n\nREPO=\"/Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS\"\nimport sys; wi=np.clip(Ki[fi],0,None)\n        ss=ws.sum(); si=wi.sum()\n        if ss>0 and si>0:\n            out[fi]=(t*ws).sum()/ss - (t*wi).sum()/si\n    return out\n\ndef band_result(b, band):\n    fit,npz,vb=R2.sources(b); ## assistant
v8's centroid-based correction is truncation-sensitive (chromatica pbf_mean=3.62ms is inflated by asymmetric window truncation of the dispersed intrinsic kernels — the fit's own t0_C is 28.16, not the implied 25.19). Every centroid-based path suffers this because the collapsed intrinsic profile is truncated asymmetrically by the residual dispersion sweep.

Let me pin down the one truncation-immune, dispersion-immune, gain-immune quantity: the **peak shift of a single Gaussian⊗PBF kernel computed on a dense zero-centered grid**. The bias in the current peak-based ToA *is* exactly this peak shift (flux-weighted over channels). Let me derive it analytically per channel and validate against the chromatica benchmark.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nimport sys; sys.path.insert(0,\"/tmp\"); import numpy as np, json\nfrom dataclasses import replace\nimport toa_recon2 as R2\nfrom scat_analysis.burstfit import analytic_gaussian_exp_convolution, gaussian_powerlaw_convolution\n\n# Peak shift of G(0,sig) (x) PBF(tau,beta), computed on a DENSE zero-centered grid.\n# Immune to data-axis truncation, dispersion sweep, gains. Function of (sig,tau,beta) only.\ndef peak_shift(sig, tau, beta, dt_fine=None):\n    span=max(20*tau, 12*sig, 2.0)\n    dt=min(sig,tau)/50.0 if dt_fine is None else dt_fine\n    dt=max(dt, span/200000)  # cap grid size\n    tg=np.arange(-span, span, dt)\n    if beta>=3.98:\n        y=analytic_gaussian_exp_convolution(tg, 0.0, sig, tau)\n    else:\n        y=gaussian_powerlaw_convolution(tg, 0.0, sig, tau, beta)\n    i=int(np.argmax(y))\n    # parabolic refine\n    if 0<i<len(y)-1:\n        d=y[i-1]-2*y[i]+y[i+1]\n        off=0.5*(y[i-1]-y[i+1])/d if d!=0 else 0.0\n    else: off=0.0\n    return (tg[i]+off*dt)   # peak position relative to Gaussian center (0)\n\ndef band_v9(b, band):\n    fit,npz,vb=R2.sources(b); d=json.load(open(fit)); p={k:v[\"median\"] for k,v in d[\"percentiles\"].items()}\n    tel=\"chime\" if band==\"C\" else \"dsa\"\n    z=np.load(npz); t=np.asarray(z[f'time{band}'],float); freq=np.asarray(z[f'freq{band}'],float)\n    npzm=np.asarray(z[f'model{band}'],float); data=np.asarray(z[f'data{band}'],float)\n    noise=z[f'noise{band}']; dt=float(np.diff(t)[0])\n    tau=float(z['tau_1ghz']); beta=float(z['beta'])\n    m=R2.FRBModel(t,freq,data=npzm,dm_init=R2._cfg_dm_init(b,tel),df_MHz=R2.NATIVE_DF[tel],beta=beta,noise_std=noise)\n    pscat,_,_,n=R2._params(d,p,band,freq,tau_override=tau,beta_override=beta)\n    # dominant component: nearest t0 to collapsed peak\n    prof_npz=np.nansum(npzm,0); use_model=(prof_npz.max()-prof_npz.min())/(abs(prof_npz.max())+1e-30)>=0.05\n 
> ```

## assistant
v9 is stable and physical across all 12 bursts — no blowups. But chromatica CHIME gives +0.851ms vs the Phase-0 benchmark of +0.36ms. That's because the NPZ chromatica fit (β=3.99, α=4.0) differs from the JSON fit I benchmarked in Phase 0 (α=3.28) — different fits, not directly comparable. Before trusting v9, I need to validate that `peak_shift` is numerically correct. Let me verify it against a direct on-axis convolution.
> **tool_use** `bash`
> ```json
> {"command": "cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS 2>/dev/null\npython - <<'PYEOF'\nimport numpy as np\nfrom scat_analysis.burstfit import analytic_gaussian_exp_convolution\n# direct check: peak of G(mu=10,sig)(x)exp(tau) minus 10 should equal dense-grid peak_shift\ndef peak_shift_dense(sig,tau,beta=4.0):\n    span=max(20*tau,12*sig,2.0); dt=min(sig,tau)/50.0; dt=max(dt,span/200000)\n    tg=np.arange(-span,span,dt); y=analytic_gaussian_exp_convolution(tg,0.0,sig,tau)\n    i=int(np.argmax(y)); d=y[i-1]-2*y[i]+y[i+1]; off=0.5*(y[i-1]-y[i+1])/d if d else 0\n    return tg[i]+off*dt\nprint(f\"{'sig':>6s}{'tau':>6s}{'ratio':>7s}{'peakshift':>11s}{'on-axis chk':>12s}\")\nfor sig,tau in [(0.3,0.54),(0.3,8.6),(0.5,3.88),(0.1,0.2),(0.3,0.06),(1.0,0.5)]:\n    ps=peak_shift_dense(sig,tau)\n    # on-axis, mu=10, fine dt\n    tg=np.arange(0,25,0.001); y=analytic_gaussian_exp_convolution(tg,10.0,sig,tau)\n    i=int(np.argmax(y)); d=y[i-1]-2*y[i]+y[i+1]; off=0.5*(y[i-1]-y[i+1])/d if d else 0\n    chk=tg[i]+off*0.001-10.0\n    print(f\"{sig:6.2f}{tau:6.2f}{tau/sig:7.1f}{ps:11.4f}{chk:12.4f}\")\n# So for tau>>sig, peak shift -> ? (exp rises instantly, peak stays near mu; small shift)\n# for tau<<sig, peak shift -> 0 (Gaussian dominates)\n# max shift around tau~sig\nPYEOF", "human_description": "Validating peak_shift against direct convolution", "environment": "flits-dev"}

Critical: v9's `peak_shift` for the **exp path (β≥3.98)** would have raised — yet v9 produced numbers for exp-PBF bursts (casey, chromatica, oran, zach, hamilton all β≈3.99). That means v9 silently caught the exception somewhere, or those went through a different path. This is exactly the kind of silent failure the reproducibility rules warn about. Let me rebuild the final engine with the **validated array-shaped** `peak_shift`, use the model's actual per-channel `sig_f`/`tau_f`, and re-run — no scalar calls.

  - [6 days ago] [observed] To land an agent's work on Faber2026 main, open a PR (branch off main via GitHub API, add files, POST /pulls) rather than committing to main directly: branch protection binds admins so direct pushes to main are rejected and a human must review+merge. PRs sit mergeable=true with mergeable_state='blocked' while awaiting the required review; 'behind' means the branch trails main and needs updating before the strict `parity` check will pass.  [mem_b203133ee982]
  - [6 days ago] [observed] When checking CI status on a commit/PR, filter by workflow name — a single commit can carry multiple workflow runs with different verdicts (e.g. FLITS commits run both `Claude Review`/`review` and `Tests`/`Python 3.12`). Reading only the first-sorted run is how earlier sessions produced false 'green' claims. `mergeable: MERGEABLE` and `mergeStateStatus: CLEAN` do not imply CI-green.  [mem_e9ff9343495d]
  - [3 hours ago] [observed] Figure 1 (fig:codetection-data-grid, the twelve-burst CHIME/DSA gallery) is governed by a fail-closed figure-review gate. Generator: scripts/plot_codetection_data_grid.py (reads analysis/dm-joint-phase-v2/manuscript_dm_catalog.csv and asserts adopted==CHIME DM to 5e-7, so it is always dedispersed to the adopted CHIME phase-coherence DMs; needs SciencePlots + flits package on PYTHONPATH=scripts:pipeline). Gate: scripts/figure_review.py verify runs in `make test-science` (Makefile:30) and fails the build if included TeX has \includegraphics of a protected target (figures/codetection_data_grid.pdf, slot fig1-gallery in figure_review/slots.json) without a matching approval receipt in figure_review/approval_receipts/. Workflow is two-PR: new-batch (stages ALL governed figures at once, reading provenance from git revisions) -> decide approved (owner name+note) -> promote (writes SHA-256 receipt) -> only then can the placeholder be swapped for the includegraphics. README states agent review …  [mem_6212be425c54]
  - [6 days ago] [observed] Faber2026 `main` has GitHub branch protection: required status check `parity` (strict/up-to-date required), 1 approving review, dismiss stale reviews, and enforce_admins=true. Because admins are bound, agent sessions can no longer self-merge their own PRs — an agent may push branches and open PRs, but a human must review and merge. This is deliberate, closing the identity-ambiguity gap at the repo level before a separate agent principal exists.  [mem_5e98b35ba938]
  - [6 days ago] [observed] Multiple Claude sessions operate on Faber2026 concurrently and all authenticate as GitHub user `jakobtfaber` committing as `Jakob Faber <jfaber@caltech.edu>` — identical to the owner, so git/PR attribution cannot tell sessions apart. The only reliable 'who did what / is anyone still active' signal is commit/PR timestamps and the `agent` tags in docs/rse/journal.jsonl. `main` has no branch protection, so the merge/review gate is not actually enforced.  [mem_e91e1470738c]

## assistant
The v10 engine is now stable and physical across all 12 bursts — every correction is positive (scattering always delays the peak), scales with τ, and CHIME ≫ DSA as the physics requires. No blowups. Here's the honest accounting of where the work stands.

## What's done and verified

**Steps 1–3 (Phase 0) — complete.**
- **t0 semantics confirmed.** The fitted `t0` is the intrinsic pre-scattering Gaussian centroid on each band's processed axis (band-top referenced). Critically, the absolute-time anchor from `io.py`'s `_centre_burst` roll is *not* recorded, so the switch must be a **peak-relative differential correction**, not an absolute-time replacement. This shaped the entire approach.
- **Scatter correction defined.** Δ_scat(band) = the peak displacement of the intrinsic Gaussian under the scattering PBF, to be subtracted from the peak-based ToA.
- **Validated on chromatica.** The differential shift is a real ~0.4–0.8 ms effect on a ~ms offset — large enough to matter, confirming the switch is worth doing.

## What just landed

**Step 4 (batch over 12 bursts) — now producing stable numbers, not yet saved as a deliverable.** This step was hard and took many iterations. The honest story:

1. I found the canonical fit source (`analysis/beta_campaign/fits/` keyed by `beta_campaign_verdicts.json`), corrected several stale suffix mappings (johndoeII is C2D2 not C2D1; hamilton→sharedzeta; zach→C2D4_cwin bespoke), and confirmed the NPZ dumps store the *true* kernel β/τ (the JSON α can be a different fit).
2. Six engine iterations failed on multi-component MARGINAL bursts (oran, phineas, isha) — OLS gain recovery on near-collinear scattered components blew up to ±160, producing nonphysical −10 ms "corrections."
3. **The resolution (v10):** compute the correction as the analytic **peak shift** of a unit Gaussian(σ_f) ⊗ PBF(τ_f, β) on a dense grid, flux-weighted over channels. This is gain-, dispersion-, component-, and truncation-immune. I validated the peak-shift function reproduces a direct on-axis convolution to 1e-4 ms.

I also caught a silent-failure trap along the way: earlier engine versions called the convolution with scalar args, which raises on the exp-PBF path — so intermediate "results" for exp bursts were suspect. v10 uses only the validated array-shaped calls.

**Current v10 output** (Δ_scat = ms to subtract from each band's peak ToA):

| burst | τ₁GHz | Δ_scat CHIME | Δ_scat DSA | differential shift |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| casey | 0.019 | +0.110 | +0.005 | +0.105 |
| chromatica | 0.220 | +0.849 | +0.049 | +0.800 |
| freya | 0.119 | +0.225 | +0.026 | +0.198 |
| wilhelm | 0.269 | +0.203 | +0.051 | +0.152 |

| oran | 0.843 | +3.139 | +0.109 | +3.031 |
| isha | 0.314 | +0.868 | +0.045 | +0.823 |

| hamilton | 0.024 | +0.040 | +0.007 | +0.034 |
| whitney | 1.182 | +0.154 | +0.060 | +0.094 |

## What has NOT been done (steps 5–12)

- **Assemble the correction table** — v10 JSON exists in `~/handoffs/`, but the CSV deliverable isn't written or saved as an artifact.
- **Prototype the new offsets** — differential shifts computed above, but not yet applied to the existing peak-bin offsets for comparison.
- **Feed model t0 into compute_toa** — no pipeline code touched yet.
- **Rebuild the error budget** — loc_err column exists (posterior t0 half-widths), not yet folded into the combined error.
- **Regenerate toa_crossmatch_results.json** — not started.
- **Update golden-JSON and alignment tests** — not started.
- **Build comparison deliverables** — not started.
- **Draft ToA methods subsection** — not started.

## One thing I want your read on before proceeding

The v10 correction is now clean, but I made a **definitional shift under the hood** during the batch that I should flag explicitly: Phase 0 locked "Δ_scat = scattered collapsed peak − intrinsic **centroid**." That centroid-based form turned out to be truncation-sensitive (the residual-DM sweep asymmetrically clips the dispersed intrinsic profile, inflating the centroid estimate). v10 instead uses the **peak shift** (scattered peak − intrinsic **peak**), which is the quantity that's actually numerically robust and is also the more literal correction to a peak-based ToA.

For a single symmetric Gaussian these differ only by the residual-DM tilt, which cancels in the differential — but they are not identical definitions.
> **tool_use** `ask_user`
> ```json

Both remove the scattering bias from the peak ToA; they differ by the residual-DM tilt term (which cancels in the CHIME\u2212DSA differential). Which should be canonical?", "header": "Correction definition", "options": [{"label": "Peak-shift (v10, Recommended)", "description": "\u0394_scat = peak of scattered kernel \u2212 peak of intrinsic Gaussian, analytic per-channel, flux-weighted. > ```

## Where the work stands

**Done (steps 1–5):**
1. ✅ t0 semantics confirmed — intrinsic centroid on the fit axis, no absolute anchor, so the switch is a peak-relative differential correction
2. ✅ Scatter correction defined — now **peak-shift** (v10, you confirmed as canonical)
3. ✅ Validated on chromatica
4. ✅ Batched over 12 bursts — stable, physical corrections after resolving the multi-component instability via the analytic per-channel peak-shift
5. ✅ Correction table assembled and saved

**The table** carries, per burst: τ₁GHz/β/α, CHIME & DSA peak ToA on the fit axis, the de-scattered model ToA, per-band scatter correction, the **differential scatter shift** applied to the offset, posterior localization errors, and both the existing peak offset and the new **model-corrected offset**. The headline result: corrections range from tens of µs (hamilton, τ tiny) to ~3 ms (oran, τ=0.84 ms), and the differential shift moves offsets by 0.03–3.0 ms — oran being the extreme case where the CHIME peak sits ~3 ms later than the true arrival.

**Remaining (steps 6–12):** prototype the new offsets into a comparison, feed model t0 into `compute_toa`, rebuild the error budget with the localization term, regenerate `toa_crossmatch_results.json`, update the golden-JSON and alignment tests, build the comparison figure/table deliverables, and draft the methods subsection.

These next steps modify pipeline code and the golden results JSON that tests assert against — the higher-risk part of the plan. Do you want me to proceed straight through steps 6–12, or pause after the offset prototype (step 6) so you can eyeball the peak-vs-model comparison before I touch `compute_toa` and the test fixtures?

If a Quantity, assumed to be seconds since the Unix\n106\t        epoch.\n107\t    offset : astropy.units.Quantity\n108\t        Instrumental or processing offset to add.\n109\t    f_center : astropy.units.Quantity\n110\t        Central observing frequency in MHz.\n111\t    DM : astropy.units.Quantity\n112\t        Dispersion measure.\n113\t    f_ref : astropy.units.Quantity\n114\t        Reference frequency in MHz.\n115\t\n116\t    Returns\n117\t    -------\n118\t    astropy.time.Time\n119\t        Time of arrival referred to ``f_ref``.\n120\t    \"\"\"\n121\t    shift = K_DM * DM.value * (1 / f_ref.value**2 - 1 / f_center.value**2) * u.s\n122\t    if isinstance(t0, Time):\n123\t        return t0 + offset + shift\n124\t    toa = t0 + offset + shift\n125\t    return Time(toa.to_value(u.s), format=\"unix\", scale=\"utc\")\n126\t\n127\t\n128\tdef compute_geometric_delay(t, src, loc1, loc2):\n129\t    \"\"\"Compute the geometric delay between two observatories.\n130\t\n131\t    Parameters\n132\t    ----------\n133\t    t : astropy.time.Time\n134\t        Time of arrival.\n135\t    src : astropy.coordinates.SkyCoord\n136\t        Source coordinates.\n137\t    loc1, loc2 : astropy.coordinates.EarthLocation\n138\t        Observatory locations.\n139\t\n140\t    Returns\n141\t    -------\n142\t    astropy.units.Quantity\n143\t        Geometric delay in milliseconds.\n144\t    \"\"\"\n145\t    p1 = loc1.get_gcrs(t).cartesian.xyz\n146\t    p2 = loc2.get_gcrs(t).cartesian.xyz\n147\t    proj = (p2 - p1).dot(src.cartesian.xyz)\n148\t    return (proj / const.c).to(u.ms)\n149\t\n150\t\n151\t

│ # Publication-Grade Figure Rules
│ 
│ *A checklist for correct, legible, internally-consistent scientific figures. This
│ skill does not impose a visual house style — frame, font, and palette are
│ parameters. Once loaded, call `apply_figure_style()` before plotting.*
│ 
│ ## §0 Scope
│ 
│ **Load trigger** (same rule as the system prompt's "Publication-grade plots"
│ section): this skill is for **final-deliverable figures** — those shipping in a
│ report, paper, or export, or saved as an artifact the user will keep — not for
│ exploratory/intermediate plots (quick looks, EDA, sanity checks), which are
│ drawn plainly without it. Once loaded, "every plot" below means every plot you
│ render toward the deliverable.
│ 
│ §1–§3, §8, and §9 are **correctness** — they apply to every plot, in every
│ context, and have no aesthetic content. §4–§7 are **guidance** — defaults that
│ produce a clean result but that a deliberate alternative can override
│ (individual rules inside §4–§7 that state a factual/perceptual invariant — e.g.
│ §4.4 semantic-zero centering, §4.5 CVD, §6.9 leader anchoring — still bind). On
